title: Resume/CV

title: The Environment of: Wastebacteria
text:
In this episode, host Kylie Keenan sits down with Trace Martin, a fourth-year PhD student at the Bren school studying wastewater treatment, coastal water quality, and fecal indicator bacteria.
Trace is also - unofficially but unanimously - the best TA ever!
Throughout the episode Trace shares his unconventional path from Santa Barbara City College to UCSB as a Geography major before becoming the youngest lab manager in the City of Santa Barbara’s wastewater lab and ultimately pursuing a PhD that bridges hands-on lab work with academic research. From microbial ecosystems inside wastewater plants, to the regulatory hurdles of approving new testing methods, this episode demystifies an often invisible system that impacts everyone.
Tune in for a fascinating look at the science behind wastewater and the people working behind the scenes to treat it.

Show Notes
Guest: Trace Martin
Our Instagram: @envionmentofitall Our LinkedIn

title: The Environment of: Industrial Decarbonization
text:
In this episode host Kylie Keenan takes a deep dive into the world of industrial decarbonization with guest Bren PhD student, Aidan O’Neil. From policy implementation to environmental justice to the origins of your favorite clean-energy beer, tune in to learn about how the industrial sector can convert to green energy and the research behind it. Join us as we take a trip behind the scenes and investigate where everyday items come from, the environmental costs they incur, and what’s being done to mitigate them.
Check out Aidan’s National Report on December 15th and his webinar with senators from Rhode Island and New Mexico on December 16th!

Show Notes

title: Ian Del Tredici
text: Welcome to my homepage! I’m Ian Del Tredici, a UCSB student pursuing degrees in Environmental Studies and Ecology & Evolution. This is my professional landing page where you can find everything from my academic coursework to my personal interests. Feel free to explore!

title: Northern Shoveler
text:
Poor Bird Guide: Northern Shoveler


When the queso cools and the pudding stiffens, you can bet your bottom dollar these waddlers will still be out plowing for glory beneath the winter sun. This is a working man’s duck (gender nonspecific), a duck that looks like a color-by-number painted by a Boy Scout. The kind of bird sat next to the antlers of a too-slow-too-fat reindeer. You need some camo pants and a sliding whistle for this birthday party. Beef jerky instead of bread crumbs if you please. This is your grandfather’s favorite duck, he just doesn’t know it. When the sky is gray and your Camry is smothered in snow, you’ll give these folks a call. They arrive in a truck that makes you question their values. Big boots, old hat, stiff smile. But don’t worry, they’re blue collar, not red collar.
And we haven’t even gotten to the star of the show yet. I mean c’mon. A certified corker and then some. The largest, flattest shnoz there ever was. They’re called spatulas for a reason. Could flip an egg with that nose. And you best believe those sneezes rock your boat. Gesundheit you marvelous scrooge. Looking like a duck from a poorly trained AI model. Add some glasses and bushy eyebrows and you’ve got yourself a third-rate comedian that can honk with the rest of ’em.
But maybe I’m just jealous. Lord knows I’d love to paddle around all day- head down, booty up as the sun shines where it was never meant to reach. And if it costs a nose that looks like the poor end of a witches curse, it might just be worth it. Paddle, eat, fly, quack, repeat. It doesn’t get much simpler than that. When you watch the ripples float off the wake of this beauty, a kind of wistfulness takes hold that’s not wholly envy or admiration but something in between. This duck is just that.























View this post on Instagram




































A post shared by @poorbirdguide

title: Canada Goose
text:
Poor Bird Guide: Canada Goose


Bird nerds and jocks alike, drop your biscuits, bow your head, and plug your ears because the Canadian monarchy has arrived. Yep, you heard that right. Saddle your horses and ready your banjos, the time of incessant honking and garbling is nigh.
While it might seem that winter has already descended upon us in all directions, no season of merriment and the same 3 songs on repeat is complete without the obnoxious, intimidating, and sometimes violent Canada goose. When these deluxe squeaky chickens arrive in your backyard you’ll know it and you’ll be continually reminded of it for the next three months. All talk and full of bite. Like Will Smith at the Oscars but with slightly more dignity.
Of course, there’s something intensely fascinating about these feathered foes. Maybe it’s their streamlined V formation that would make the Blue Angels jealous or their tendency to assume we’ve created golf courses as their personal toilets. Or perhaps it’s just the immense size of them. Like a sack of potatoes on two legs some of them. But whatever it is you can’t deny that these beasts have the confidence and arrogance of a frat bro out way past his bedtime. The fearsome strut of a slicked-backed-hair, leather jacket-wearing, cliche high school bully in an early-2000s Disney show. Hold on to your lunch money kids.
And yet, nothing gets you quite ready for a nice cuppa hot cocoa in front of a cozy fire like these yapping jocks. Gaze out past the fog, admire their tenacity, and burn your tongue on a scalding mug of apple cider. This is what the holidays are all about.
























View this post on Instagram




































A post shared by @poorbirdguide

title: About Me
text:
I grew up in classy San Diego with my younger sister (2 years, 2 days) and my lovely parents. Like many children of that time I spent my days running around outside, reading Harry Potter, playing sports, and sprinting home after school to play Mario Kart. From the age of 6 I fell in love with soccer, signing my parents up for practices twice week and weekends devoted to driving endlessly for a 2 hour game. When I wasn’t out on the field or buried deep in a book, my dad would take us out on hikes, stoking the early embers of my environmental passions that I carry today.
My mother, then, is the source of my love of science. To me she has always been ” a scientist”, donning a lab coat and goggles before swirling chemicals in a flask. Even days spent playing in her office, couldn’t sway that illusion. Now, of course, I can put a name to her job, which has in turn inspired my fixation with science writing.
Those early years, all of us sat around the dinner table discussing whatever math problem or science concept either of my parents had introduced, are core memories at the foundation of who I am today.

title: About Me
text:
Beyond the classroom

My hobbies and interests have been much like a bee in a flower garden. Buzzing intermittently between flowers, landing briefly but never staying long. It is a garden, by my estimation, of odd pairings and a hodgepodge assortment. As a kid it was Pokemon and Harry Potter, until it became drawing and martial arts before switching to writing and ukulele. Now I’ve transitioned to Formula One and bird watching with the occasionally intrusion of cooking, tide pooling, and crocheting.

That said, there are a few passions that have gripped my attention all the while. Soccer was an early one, sparking fond memories of Saturday mornings spent watching Bundesliga on the couch with my dad.



First camping trip with my sister (left)


Reading, of course is a constant, as is my love of camping. For many years our little caravan would make the short journey up into apple country (Julian, CA) to spend a weekend among the oaks and beneath the stars before coming home with an apple pie in our hands.

title: About Me
text:
Career Goals
I have never been one to doggedly pursue a specific image of what I want my future to be. Rather, I have dove deep into the things that spark my interest, allowing their currents to take me onto the next. This method has been both a hindrance and a propellant of my career aspirations, at once obscuring the long term future but also enabling me to focus steadfastly on the things before me. As I have explored, discovered, and grown at UCSB that hazy future has begun to show glimpses of clarity.
In the near term I have my sights set on graduate school, hoping to obtain a PhD in environmental science or evolutionary biology. With that, of course, comes the understanding that a doctorate is not the end goal but another tool in the tool belt for meaningful impact. The work I hope to achieve will have a tangible, purposeful impact on our understanding and appreciation of the natural world.
For now, I am content with supporting the work of current PhD students as an intern, watching and learning from what I hope will be my future. My research interests range from the detailed world of environmental communication to the broader impacts of environmental policy and its relation to everyday lives. At Bren I have worked with PhD students Mukta Kelkar (now Dr. Kelkar!) and Cali Pfleger on their projects centered on the interface between environmental technologies and public perception.



Map of CHNMS boundary


Analysis of public comments from the Chumash National Marine Sanctuary (CHNMS) is set to be published at a later date, while another project analyzing news headlines on geoengineering technologies is still in progress. Both of them have been invaluable mentors, graciously pulling back the curtain on the research process and life as a graduate student. Any work I eventually produce will be deeply indebted on their generous guidance.

title: Great Blue Heron
text:
Poor Bird Guide: Great Blue Heron


I cannot believe I got a picture of this bird. This is the equivalent of finding LeBron James in your backyard. Or Harry Styles. Just pure royalty. The great blue heron is the king of all birds, and it knows it. The eye liner, the blue cap, the posture, everything just screams I’m better than you and there’s nothing you can do about it. I truly can’t believe nature kept popping out birds after this one. Like just give up at this point. You did it. You created a pristine specimen. You can’t beat it.
If you ever stop and watch a Great Blue Heron for a while (highly recommend) like your sister watches The Bachelor (no makeup, pizza rolls, full commitment) you’ll notice just how much patience and poise this bird has when hunting. I kid you not this bird could be a 3rd grade substitute teacher and those chums would be sitting on their tiny desks doing multiplication tables by 10am. Just a bird that exudes serenity. This bird that will watch a full soccer game, no breaks, no snacks, not even a twitch towards its phone at the 60th minute. Professors wish this bird would come to their classes.
All to say that anytime you see this bird just remember how high the totem pool of perfection reaches above you. You are a leftover pencil shaving compared to this heron. And not even a colored pencil either, just a plain old #3 pencil, if those even exist. Out there on the pond, where the soft sea meets the low grass, there’s nothing more regarded than a Great Blue Heron.
























View this post on Instagram




































A post shared by @poorbirdguide

title: A personal data project from ENVS 193DS
text:
This is a sample of my code from a personal data project in my Environmental Statistics class. I collected data on how much time I spent reading each genre over the course of 5 weeks before plotting it and running a few tests. The BIG Question: Does reading genre affect how long I read?

Step 1: Load in libraries and data

library(tidyverse) #load in packages
library(here)
library(janitor)
library(readxl)
library(ggeffects)
reading_data <- read.csv( #read in personal data using here function
  here("data", "time_spent_reading.csv"))



Step 2: Clean up the data frame

reading_data <- reading_data |>  #use reading data frame
  clean_names() |> #clean column names
  rename("reading_time" = "time_spent_reading_min", #rename columns
         "genre" = "reading_type")



Step 3: Create a boxplot comparing the median reading session length of each genre

ggplot(data = reading_data, #create base layer with personal data
       aes(x = genre, #set x axis
           y = reading_time, #set y axis
           color = genre, #color by genre
           fill = genre)) + #fill by genre
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.6, #create box plot and set width
               alpha = 0.2, #set transparency of box plot
               outliers = FALSE) + #remove outliers 
  geom_jitter(height = 0, #create jitter plot and restrict vertical jitter
              width = 0.2) + #set horizontal jitter
  labs(title = "Length of reading session per genre", #plot title
       x = "Genre", #x axis title
       y = "Time spent reading (min)", #y axis title
       subtitle = "March 1, 2026") + #subtitle
  theme_minimal() + #set theme to minimal
  scale_color_manual(values = c( #change colors manually
    "Nonfiction Novels" = "darkgreen",
    "Research Paper" = "darkorchid",
    "News" = "coral2"))




Figure 1. There is no difference in length of reading session across genre. Each genre contains a box plot displaying the median reading length, upper and lower quartiles, and minimum and maximum. Outliers are hid for visual clarity but each genre’s highest point is classified as an outlier. Individual observations are layered on as data points. Genres are separated by color (pink: News, green: Nonfiction Novels, purple: Research Paper). All genres overlap with nearly identical medians. Data collected from January 6, 2026 to March 1, 2026 by Ian Del Tredici.






Step 4: Create a scatterplot of the relationship between the number of pages read and reading length by genre

ggplot(data = reading_data, #create plot with personal data
       aes(x = number_of_pages,#set x axis
           y = reading_time, #set y axis
           color = genre)) + #separate by color and genre
  geom_point(size = 3) + #add in individual data and adjust size
  labs(title = "Reading session per number of pages", #add title
       x = "Number of pages", #set x axis title
       y = "Reading time (min)",#set y axis title
       subtitle = "March 1, 2026") + 
  theme_gray() + #change theme
  scale_color_manual(values = c( #change colors manually
    "Nonfiction Novels" = "darkgreen",
    "Research Paper" = "darkorchid",
    "News" = "coral2")) +
  facet_wrap(~genre) + #separate by genre
  theme(legend.position = "none") #remove legend




Figure 2. As the number of pages increases, reading time also increases. Points represent individual reading sessions with time of session (min) and number of pages recorded. Colors represent the three types of reading genres (coral: News, green: Nonfiction Novels, and blue: Research Paper). Panels also divide genres. Data collected from January 6, 2026 to March 1, 2026 by Ian Del Tredici.

title: The Environment of: The Frog Fungus
text:
In this episode, hosts Jasmine Schwam and Kylie Keenan speak with Cannon Mallory, a fourth year PhD candidate at Bren studying disease ecology and the devastating frog fungus known as Batrachochytrium dendrobatidis (Bd). Throughout this episode Cannon delves into the mystery of this deadly disease- how it works, how it moves through an environment, and why its effects differ between populations. Cannon walks us through his journey from engineer to herpetologist while also discussing some of the most fascinating amphibian anomalies to date. Join us as we learn more about everyone’s favorite green little critters and why pseudacris cadaverina is the frog to be.

Show Notes
Guest: Cannon Mallory
Our Instagram: @envionmentofitall Our LinkedIn

title: The Environment of It All Podcast
text:
The Environment of It All
In late 2024 I joined the Environment of It All podcast, a student-run podcast dedicated to bridging the gap between academia and the public. Each episode features a different graduate student at UCSB, exploring their work, how they got here, and all the ways research takes place. From forest ecology to wastewater treatment and everything in between we make research exciting, appealing, and approachable to anyone who will listen.
I came on primarily as a writing intern, helping edit episodes and drafting short blurbs about each guest. Since then I have gradually moved up, becoming assistant producer in 2025 before graduating fully to executive producer. As the manager of our 6 lovely team members I am responsible for coordinating interviews, organizing team meetings, and keeping everybody on track. I sit in on editing sessions, oversee public events, and generally provide a structure for the rest of the team to perform. As I’ve grown into this role I’ve seen my leadership skills develop immensely while also learning a great deal about each topic. One of my favorite things is to dive deep into the research of a guest, exploring their topic and brainstorming interesting questions. It’s such a delight to watch their eyes gleam as they allow us into their passions.
See some of our most recent episodes below!














The Environment of: Industrial Decarbonization


Follow along as Aidan O’Neil explores how industry can aid in the climate crisis!






Dec 8, 2025













The Environment of: The Frog Fungus


Enter the wild world of frog fungi with guest Cannon Mallory as he seeks to understand the deadliest plauge among amphibians






Aug 5, 2025













The Environment of: Wastebacteria


Listen to Trace Martin distill the wonders of our wastewater treatment system!






Feb 11, 2026
